metadata.title: Gap Analysis: What's Between You and That Job (20 pts)
metadata.playlist: Plan It Like a PM — Your Madison PRD
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 2 brief — Plan Your Madison Project Like a Pro

beats.narration_text: Hola — this is Liam. Episode 2. The gap analysis is the four-column table that turns 'I'm not qualified yet' into an actual plan — and into your Madison project. The four columns are: They Want. I Have. Gap to Fill. Madison Could Help By. By the end of this video you have a real table built from the posting you found in Episode 1, with honest entries in every column and a specific Madison layer mapped to each gap. Let's build it.

props.segment: Gap Analysis: What's Between You and That Job
props.command: Build the four-column gap analysis from your Episode 1 posting.
props.runningText: drafting gap table — They Want | I Have | Gap to Fill | Madison Could Help By…
output: They Want | I Have | Gap to Fill | Madison Could Help By…
3+ real, job-specific gaps — not generic ones
Madison mapped honestly to each gap

beats.narration_text: Let's define each column precisely, because vague columns produce vague tables. Column one, They Want: pull directly from the top three technical requirements you extracted in Episode 1 — in the company's exact words. Do not rewrite them here. Column two, I Have: be accurate. Not 'I'm learning Python' if you have done two tutorials. Write what you can actually demonstrate to an interviewer today — a project, a course with deliverables, a deployment. Column three, Gap to Fill: name the specific skill, credential, or portfolio piece that is missing. 'No production experience deploying ML models' is a gap. 'Need more AI experience' is not — it has no edges and no fix. Column four, Madison Could Help By: one concrete action per gap, tied to a specific Madison layer or component. 'Madison will help somehow' will lose points. Name the layer and name the action.

props.artifactHeading: Four columns — what each one means
artifactLines: They Want — the posting's exact technical requirements, verbatim from Episode 1. No rewriting.
I Have — what you can demonstrate today: a project, a deployment, a course with a real deliverable.
Gap to Fill — specific and bounded: 'no production RAG pipeline experience,' not 'need more AI skills.'
Madison Could Help By — one concrete action + a specific Madison layer/component. No vague mapping.
props.sparkLine: Vague columns produce vague tables. Be specific.

beats.act: how to be honest about I Have
beats.narration_text: The 'I Have' column is where students lose points, because there are two failure modes — over-claiming and under-claiming — and both produce a bad project. Over-claiming gives you a gap analysis that says you're almost ready, so the Madison project has nothing real to close. Under-claiming gives you a project that's too remedial to be interesting at the master's level. Use this self-audit prompt. It takes one skill from the posting, your actual honest experience with it, and returns a calibrated one-sentence description of your current level plus what you'd need to do to reach the posting's standard. Run it for each of the three technical requirements. The output goes directly into the 'I Have' column.

props.command: Here is a skill from a job posting: [SKILL]. Here is what I can honestly say I have done with it: [YOUR ACTUAL EXPERIENCE — be specific: courses, projects, time used]. Give me one sentence that accurately describes my current level — not modest, not inflated. Then tell me what I would need to do to move from that level to the posting's requirement.
props.runningText: calibrating — be specific about what you've actually done…
output: One calibrated sentence: your honest current level
What you would need to do to reach the posting's standard
Run once per technical requirement from the posting

beats.narration_text: Now for the Madison column — and here the honesty rule matters most. Madison is an open-source agent-based AI marketing framework with five layers. It is not a magic answer for every gap. Pick the layer and component that actually fits the gap. Do not stretch. The five layers are: Intelligence, which includes MarketMind Research for secondary market and competitor research; Content, which includes Brand Voice Personalization via NLP and content creation and distribution workflows; Research, which covers survey analysis and customer insight; Experience, which includes the AI Concierge Systems and customer journey design; and Performance, which covers the Multi-Armed Bandit and Thompson sampling components for campaign A/B testing and measurement. The two named integrations are Bellman, which handles reinforcement learning, and Popper, which handles evidence-based validation and bias detection. One more thing before we go on: Madison is the design framework. n8n — a separate visual workflow tool — is what students use to actually build the automations in this course. They are paired in the assignment but they are not the same product. Do not write 'Madison will automate this via n8n' as if they are one system — they are not.

props.artifactTitle: Madison layers — which one fits your gap
props.artifactHeading: Madison layers — which one fits your gap
artifactLines: Intelligence layer — MarketMind Research: secondary market research, competitor analysis.
Content layer — Brand Voice Personalization (NLP), content creation and distribution.
Research layer — survey analysis, customer insight gathering.
Experience layer — AI Concierge Systems, customer journey mapping.
Performance layer — Multi-Armed Bandit / Thompson sampling for A/B tests and measurement.
Integrations: Bellman (reinforcement learning), Popper (evidence-based validation, bias detection).
IMPORTANT: Madison ≠ n8n. Madison is the design framework. n8n is the build tool. They are separate products.
props.sparkLine: Pick the layer that fits. Do not stretch.

beats.act: the excellence move — research the company tech stack
beats.narration_text: The excellence move for this section: research the company's actual technology stack before you write the 'They Want' and 'Madison Could Help By' columns. A gap analysis that names the company's real tools — rather than generic AI tools — shows research. Use this prompt to surface what the company actually uses. Then verify every tool it names before you put it in your table. The AI might hallucinate a tool the company does not use — check the company's GitHub profile, their engineering blog, or their job postings for other roles. If you cannot verify a tool through a primary source, do not claim it. Write what you can confirm.

props.segment: The excellence move — company tech stack
props.command: I'm researching [COMPANY NAME] for a job application. Based on their job postings, GitHub, engineering blog, and any public conference talks, what specific tools, frameworks, and AI/ML platforms do they use? I want to name specific technologies in my gap analysis.
output: Specific tools and frameworks the company uses — from public sources
Verify each tool against company GitHub, engineering blog, or job postings before including it
Do not claim a tool you cannot confirm through a primary source

beats.narration_text: Now build the table on the Figma board. Add a new section — label it 'Part 2 — Gap Analysis.' Create a table frame with four columns and at least four rows: one header row and at least three gap rows. Use auto-layout so text does not overflow the cells — if you do not use auto-layout, text will spill outside the frame and the board will look broken. Label each column header in your brand font: They Want, I Have, Gap to Fill, Madison Could Help By. Then fill one row at a time, starting with your strongest gap — the one where the distance between 'I Have' and 'They Want' is largest and most specific. That gap is your Madison project.

artifactLines: 1. Add a new Figma section: label it 'Part 2 — Gap Analysis.'
2. Create a table frame: 4 columns, 4+ rows (header + 3 gap rows minimum).
3. Apply auto-layout to every cell — prevents text overflow on the board.
4. Label column headers in your brand font: They Want | I Have | Gap to Fill | Madison Could Help By.
5. Fill one row at a time; start with your largest, most specific gap — that gap is your Madison project.
props.sparkLine: Auto-layout on every cell. Text overflow kills the review.

beats.narration_text: Four traps for the gap analysis. Trap one: generic gaps. 'Need more AI experience' has no edges and no fix — you cannot measure it and you cannot close it. 'No production experience with RAG pipelines in a marketing context' is a gap. Name the specific thing you have not done yet. Trap two: vague Madison mapping. 'Madison will help somehow' will lose you points on a twenty-point section. Every gap needs a specific Madison layer and a concrete action — for example, 'Experience layer: build an AI Concierge System prototype to demonstrate customer journey personalization.' Trap three: no connection to the Episode 1 job. The table must map to requirements in the posting you chose. If the gaps in your table could apply to any job in any industry, you have done the analysis at the wrong level of specificity. Trap four: inflating 'I Have.' The instructor can tell, and an inflated self-assessment builds a project that doesn't close the real gap — which means your PRD won't be coherent with your goals.

artifactLines: Trap 1 — Generic gaps: 'need more AI experience' has no fix; name the specific skill or experience missing.
Trap 2 — Vague Madison mapping: name the layer and a concrete action — not 'Madison will help somehow.'
Trap 3 — Not tied to Episode 1: every gap must trace to a requirement in your actual posting.
Trap 4 — Inflated I Have: an inflated self-assessment builds a project that can't close the real gap.
props.sparkLine: Specific gaps. Specific Madison. Tied to the real posting.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for Gap Analysis. Three or more gaps, each tied to a real technical requirement from the Episode 1 posting. Exact column labels — They Want, I Have, Gap to Fill, Madison Could Help By. Honest entries in I Have — not modest, not inflated, demonstrably accurate. Madison mapped to a specific layer and a concrete action for each gap. And the excellence move: the company's real technology stack named in the They Want column, verified through a primary source. Remember: Madison is the design framework. n8n is the build tool. They work together in this assignment, but they are separate products — do not conflate them in your table. And confirm the assignment deadline with your instructor before you submit: the brief has a date conflict between the header and the submission block.

props.artifactTitle: Gap Analysis: What's Between You and That Job
artifactLines: 3+ gaps — each tied to a real technical requirement from the Episode 1 posting.
Exact column labels: They Want | I Have | Gap to Fill | Madison Could Help By.
Honest I Have: demonstrably accurate — not modest, not inflated.
Madison mapping: specific layer + concrete action per gap. No vague entries.
Excellence: company's real tech stack in They Want, verified through a primary source.
Madison ≠ n8n: Madison is the design framework; n8n is the build tool — separate products.
Deadline conflict in the brief — confirm the real submission date with your instructor.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Take the top three technical requirements from the job posting you found in Episode 1 and write down your honest current level with each one — specific: courses you completed, projects you shipped, tools you actually used, time you spent with them. Then paste this prompt into Claude with all three requirements and all three honest levels filled in. When the gap table comes back, tighten every Madison mapping that looks like a stretch — the model will sometimes pick the nearest-sounding layer rather than the correct one. Check it against the five-layer list in this episode. If any gap maps to Madison vaguely, rewrite that row yourself. The table is your work and your responsibility — Claude drafts, you own the truth.

props.segment: Gap Analysis: What's Between You and That Job
props.command: Here are the top 3 requirements from my target job posting: [REQ 1], [REQ 2], [REQ 3]. For each, here is my honest current level: [LEVEL 1], [LEVEL 2], [LEVEL 3]. Draft a 4-column gap analysis table: They Want | I Have | Gap to Fill | Madison Could Help By (assign each gap to a specific Madison layer — Intelligence, Content, Research, Experience, or Performance — and name one concrete action). Flag any gap where the Madison mapping feels like a stretch.
props.runningText: drafting — then tighten each Madison mapping…

beats.narration_text: Gap Analysis: What's Between You and That Job. Liam, in for Bear.
